name: debugging
context: Debug Node.js applications with the inspector protocol, Chrome DevTools, VS Code, and diagnostic tools
info: Node.js ships with a built-in inspector that speaks the Chrome DevTools Protocol over WebSocket. The `--inspect` flag starts the inspector on port 9229, letting you attach Chrome DevTools, VS Code, or any CDP-compatible debugger. Beyond breakpoint debugging, Node.js provides diagnostic reports for post-mortem analysis, heap snapshots for memory leak detection, CPU profiling with `--cpu-prof`, and async stack traces for tracing promise chains. The `console` object offers far more than `console.log` — table formatting, timers, grouping, and trace. The `NODE_DEBUG` environment variable enables debug output from core modules. For production diagnostics, tools like clinic.js and 0x provide flame graphs and event loop analysis. Every agent that runs Node.js code needs to understand these tools to diagnose failures, performance issues, and memory leaks.
triggers: find a memory leak in node

sections.title: V8 Inspector Protocol
sections.body: Start the inspector with `node --inspect` (non-blocking) or `node --inspect-brk` (pause on first line). The inspector listens on `ws://127.0.0.1:9229` by default. Connect via Chrome DevTools (`chrome://inspect`), VS Code debugger, or any Chrome DevTools Protocol client. To debug a running process, send `SIGUSR1` to enable the inspector: `kill -USR1 <pid>`. The inspector supports breakpoints, stepping, call stack inspection, variable watches, and expression evaluation.

sections.title: Chrome DevTools and VS Code
sections.body: Chrome DevTools: open `chrome://inspect`, configure network targets if needed, click 'inspect' on the listed Node.js target. VS Code: create a `.vscode/launch.json` with `type: 'node'` configurations. Use `request: 'launch'` to start debugging or `request: 'attach'` to connect to a running inspector. Set `skipFiles: ['<node_internals>/**']` to skip Node.js internal frames. The `restart: true` option enables auto-reconnect on server restart.

sections.title: Advanced console methods
sections.body: Beyond `console.log`: `console.table()` formats arrays and objects as tables. `console.time()`/`console.timeEnd()` measures execution duration. `console.count()` counts invocations. `console.trace()` prints stack traces. `console.group()`/`console.groupEnd()` nests output. `console.dir(obj, { depth: null })` inspects objects beyond default depth. `console.assert(condition, message)` logs only on failure. `util.debuglog('section')` creates loggers that only output when `NODE_DEBUG=section`.

sections.title: Diagnostic reports and heap snapshots
sections.body: Diagnostic reports (`--report-on-fatalerror`, `process.report.writeReport()`) generate JSON files with Node.js version, heap stats, native stack, libuv handles, and environment. Heap snapshots (`v8.writeHeapSnapshot()`) capture the full JavaScript heap for analysis in Chrome DevTools. Compare two snapshots to find memory leaks. Use `--max-old-space-size` to set heap limits and `--expose-gc` to manually trigger garbage collection during debugging.

sections.title: CPU and performance profiling
sections.body: Use `--cpu-prof` to generate V8 CPU profiles (`.cpuprofile` files) viewable as flame charts in Chrome DevTools or VS Code. The lower-level `--prof` flag generates tick-processor logs that `--prof-process` converts to human-readable output. Third-party tools like `0x` generate interactive flame graphs and `clinic.js` provides Doctor (event loop), Flame (CPU), and Bubbleprof (async) analysis modes. Always profile under realistic load — use tools like `autocannon` or `wrk` to generate traffic.

related.note: Understanding Node.js runtime basics is essential before debugging
